did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses deep Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), specifically InceptionV3 and ResNet50 architectures with customized fully connected layers, to perform binary melanoma classification (distinguishing melanoma from nevus) on the ISIC 2019 dataset. The results were obtained by optimizing these AI models and comparing their performance against dermatologists on the MClass-D dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and optimization of deep Convolutional Neural Networks (InceptionV3 and ResNet50) for binary melanoma classification. It describes specific methods (custom fully connected layers, best model selection based on Youden Index), uses the ISIC 2019 dataset for training and the MClass-D dataset for validation, and reports new performance results comparing the proposed AI models against 157 dermatologists.

title: Improving binary skin cancer classification based on best model selection method combined with optimizing full connected layers of Deep CNN
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of binary melanoma classification by proposing an optimized deep Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) architecture combined with a best model selection method based on the Youden Index. The researchers utilized the ISIC 2019 dataset, comprising 17,302 dermoscopic images of melanoma and nevus, to train and select the best models using InceptionV3 and ResNet50 architectures with customized fully connected layers. The performance of these optimized models was evaluated on the test-10 subset of ISIC 2019 and compared against the diagnostic performance of 157 dermatologists on the MClass-D dataset of 100 images. The proposed max-sen-spe model selection method achieved an AUC of 94.3% on test-10 and 88.4% on MClass-D, effectively solving underfitting and overfitting issues. The AI models outperformed 153 out of 157 dermatologists, surpassing the previous state-of-the-art solution by 17 dermatologists and demonstrating superior sensitivity and specificity balance.

lesion_types_studied.details: Malignant skin cancer; one of the two classes in the binary classification task. The dataset contained 4,503 melanoma images (minority class) out of 17,302 total images used for training/validation/testing.

Benign skin lesion; the other class in the binary classification task. The dataset contained 12,799 nevus images (majority class). The MClass-D dataset used for dermatologist comparison contained 80 nevi.

all_task_types.details: The paper performs binary classification to distinguish between melanoma and nevus skin lesions using deep Convolutional Neural Networks (InceptionV3 and ResNet50). The model outputs a probability via a sigmoid function, which is converted to a discrete class (positive/negative) using a threshold (typically 0.5). Performance is evaluated using classification metrics such as Accuracy, Sensitivity (SEN), Specificity (SPE), and Area Under the Curve (AUC).

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2019 Challenge Train Dataset
datasets_used.description: The latest and largest public image collection about skin cancer at the time, consisting of dermoscopic images with diagnoses confirmed by histopathological evaluation. The study filtered this dataset to include only melanoma and nevus images for binary classification.

use.training: Used for training the deep CNN models (InceptionV3 and ResNet50) with 150 epochs.
use.validation: Used for validation (10% of the filtered dataset) to assist in model selection.
use.testing: Used for testing (test-10 subset, 10% of the filtered dataset) to evaluate the performance of the selected best models.

datasets_used.name: MClass-D Dataset
datasets_used.description: A dataset of 100 dermoscopic images published by Titus J. Brinker et al., used as a benchmark to compare the performance of the proposed AI models against 157 dermatologists from 12 university hospitals in Germany.

use.testing: Used for external validation and comparison of the best models' performance against human dermatologists.
use.other: Used as a benchmark for comparing AI diagnostic performance with certified dermatologists.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: Used as a custom metric for best model selection to balance sensitivity and specificity, and to compare the performance of the proposed AI models against dermatologists.
Used as a custom metric for best model selection to address class imbalance in the binary melanoma classification task.
Used to evaluate the diagnostic performance of the deep CNN models and to compare them with the performance of 157 dermatologists on the MClass-D dataset.
Used to evaluate the effectiveness of the binary skin cancer classification system and to compare AI models with dermatologists, particularly in the context of the Youden Index calculation.
Used to thoroughly analyze the system's performance by examining critical thresholds and comparing the performance of best models on the test-10 and MClass-D datasets.
Used to compare the aggregate performance (AUC, SEN, SPE) of the AI models against the mean performance of 157 dermatologists from 12 university hospitals.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, t-tests, or significance levels). Performance comparisons between AI models and dermatologists were based on descriptive statistics such as AUC, Sensitivity, Specificity, and Youden Index, without statistical significance testing.

ISIC 2019 (test-10 subset).description: Evaluation of 12 best models (6 InceptionV3, 6 ResNet50) selected via different metrics on the test-10 dataset (1,730 images: 450 melanoma, 1,280 nevus) using a threshold of 0.5.
ISIC 2019 (test-10 subset).notes: InceptionV3 with max-sen-spe achieved the highest AUC (94.3%). Max-sen and max-bacc models handled class imbalance well with low Delta values. Traditional methods (last-model, max-acc) had high Delta values indicating imbalance.

MClass-D Dataset.description: Evaluation of 12 best models on 100 dermoscopic images (20 melanoma, 80 nevus) using a threshold of 0.5. Compared against 157 dermatologists.
MClass-D Dataset.notes: The InceptionV3 max-sen-spe model achieved the highest AUC (88.4%) and outperformed 153 out of 157 dermatologists. The ResNet50 max-sen-spe model achieved an AUC of 86.1% and outperformed 140 out of 157 doctors. Max-sen and max-bacc models showed reduced difference between sensitivity and specificity compared to traditional methods.

Threshold Analysis (test-10).description: Performance at specific SEN/SPE thresholds compared to SOTA and dermatologists.
Threshold Analysis (test-10).notes: The proposed max-sen-spe method allows for an optimized threshold of 85% SEN and 83.8% SPE, balancing sensitivity and specificity while maintaining high accuracy.

architecture_used.main_model: Deep Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN), specifically InceptionV3 and ResNet50 architectures
architecture_used.pretraining: Not explicitly reported in the provided context (standard practice for these architectures implies ImageNet pretraining, but not stated)

architecture_details.custom_layers: Customized fully connected layers (FC) replacing the original FC layers of the backbone architectures
architecture_details.fc_structure: Two hidden layers: first layer with 1,024 nodes, second layer with 512 nodes
architecture_details.dropout: Dropout rate of 0.5 used to avoid overfitting

optimizer_hyperparameters.learning_rate_schedule: Cyclical learning rate (changed after each epoch)

training_details.loss_function: Not explicitly reported (standard binary cross-entropy implied for binary classification with sigmoid output)
training_details.dataset: ISIC 2019 Challenge Train Dataset (filtered to 17,302 images: 13,842 training, 1,730 validation, 1,730 testing)

classification_or_output_head.type: Fully connected layer with sigmoid activation
classification_or_output_head.output: Real number in range [0,1] representing probability
classification_or_output_head.threshold: 0.5 (default), with analysis of other critical thresholds

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Brinker et al. deep CNN system (2019)
conclusions: The proposed best model selection method based on maximizing the Youden Index (max-sen-spe) effectively solves the underfitting problem and avoids overfitting in binary melanoma classification.

The max-sen-spe method outperforms traditional model selection methods (such as last-model and max-acc) by increasing the Youden Index and minimizing the difference between sensitivity and specificity on both the test-10 and MClass-D datasets.

InceptionV3 architecture combined with the max-sen-spe selection method achieved the highest AUC of 94.3% on the test-10 dataset, making it the best solution for melanoma binary image classification among the tested models.
On the MClass-D dataset, the InceptionV3 (max-sen-spe) model achieved an AUC of 88.4% and outperformed 153 out of 157 dermatologists.
The ResNet50 (max-sen-spe) model achieved an AUC of 86.1% on the MClass-D dataset and outperformed 140 out of 157 dermatologists.
The proposed solution surpasses the current state-of-the-art solution by outperforming 17 more dermatologists than the previous best model.

Models using max-sen and max-bacc selection methods effectively handled class imbalance by maintaining low differences between sensitivity and specificity, particularly with the ResNet50 architecture where the difference was reduced to 2.5%.

Traditional methods (last-model and max-acc) resulted in high differences between sensitivity and specificity (up to 52.5% with InceptionV3), indicating poor balance in handling the minority class (melanoma).
important_remarks: The study relies on a small external validation dataset (MClass-D, n=100) for comparison with dermatologists, which limits the statistical power of the human-AI comparison.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values) was performed to compare AI performance against dermatologists; comparisons were based on descriptive statistics (AUC, SEN, SPE).
The model selection process used the validation set from the ISIC 2019 dataset, which may introduce selection bias or optimistic performance estimates compared to a completely held-out test set.

The study addresses class imbalance (4,503 melanoma vs. 12,799 nevus images) by proposing a custom model selection metric (Youden Index) rather than using standard data augmentation or sampling techniques, which may limit generalizability to other imbalance scenarios.
The comparison with dermatologists used a specific dataset (MClass-D) published by Brinker et al., which may not represent the full diversity of clinical practice or other public datasets.

The authors acknowledge that the experiment was limited to one customized fully connected layer structure, suggesting that the findings may not generalize to other architectural configurations without further intensive study.

The study does not report data leakage risks explicitly, but the use of the same dataset (ISIC 2019) for training, validation, and internal testing (test-10) requires careful interpretation of generalizability to external populations.
